column1: #separator:tab
[Sum of Two Integers] What two bit operations replace addition, and why must you loop?
column2: XOR gives sum without carry; (a & b) << 1 gives the carry. Loop because XOR+AND produce two new numbers still needing addition — stop when carry is zero.

column1: [Sum of Two Integers] What does (a & b) << 1 compute and why?
column2: The carry — AND finds positions where both bits are 1 (carry occurs), left shift places it in the correct next column.

column1: [Counting Bits] Count set bits for all numbers 0..n in O(n): what recurrence avoids recomputing?
column2: bits[i] = 1 + bits[i - p], where p is the largest power of 2 <= i. Each section [2^k, 2^(k+1)-1] mirrors the previous with every value +1.

column1: [Counting Bits] Why does the pattern repeat at powers of 2 and not at constant intervals?
column2: Each power of 2 adds a new leading 1-bit — the result is an exact mirror of the previous section. This follows binary representation, not arithmetic spacing.

column1: [Reverse Bits] To reverse a 32-bit integer bit by bit: what two operations per iteration and in what order?
column2: Left-shift revNum first (make room), OR in n & 1 (append LSB), then right-shift n. Repeat 32 times.

column1: [Reverse Bits] What is the key shift direction rule — input vs output?
column2: Input (n) right-shifts to expose each bit; output (revNum) left-shifts to build the reversed number — they go in opposite directions.

column1: [Insert Interval] Three cases for each existing interval when inserting a new one — what are they and what do you do?
column2: Before (cur.end < new.start) → add cur. After (cur.start > new.end) → flush new first, then add cur. Overlap → expand new: start = min(both), end = max(both).

column1: [Insert Interval] What trailing edge case must you handle after the loop?
column2: If newInterval was never flushed (no 'after' case was ever hit), add it at the end of the result.

column1: [Merge Intervals] Core approach: why sort first, what is the merge condition, and how do you extend?
column2: Sort by start so overlapping intervals are adjacent. Merge when cur.start <= last result end. Extend: last.end = max(last.end, cur.end) — not just cur.end, in case cur is fully contained.

column1: [Merge Intervals] Why use max(prevEnd, curEnd) when extending, not just curEnd?
column2: The current interval may be fully contained inside the last merged one — curEnd could be smaller than prevEnd and would incorrectly shrink it.

column1: [Non-Overlapping Intervals] To minimise removals: when two intervals overlap, which do you keep and why?
column2: Keep the one with the smaller end — it conflicts with fewer future intervals (greedy earliest end). Remove the larger-end one.

column1: [Non-Overlapping Intervals] Why is no deletion needed, and what is the exact overlap condition?
column2: Only the count matters — track prevEnd virtually. Overlap: cur.start < prevEnd (strict less than; touching intervals like [1,2],[2,3] are not overlapping).

column1: [Meeting Rooms II] Find minimum rooms needed: what is the O(n log n) min-heap approach?
column2: Sort by start. Min-heap stores end times. For each meeting: if heap.top() <= cur.start, reuse that room (pop, push new end). Else push new end. Answer = heap size.

column1: [Meeting Rooms II] Why does a vector of rooms give O(n²) while a min-heap gives O(n log n)?
column2: Finding the earliest-ending room in a vector requires scanning all of them — O(n) per meeting. A min-heap gives O(log n) access to the minimum end time.

column1: [Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock] Single buy-sell for max profit: what O(n) O(1) approach works?
column2: Track minPrice seen so far. At each price: maxProfit = max(maxProfit, price - minPrice). Update minPrice each step.

column1: [Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock] Why initialise maxProfit to 0 rather than INT_MIN?
column2: Not transacting is always valid — 0 profit is achievable. INT_MIN would force a trade even when all prices fall.

column1: [Maximum Subarray — Kadane's] At each element, what is the decision and the recurrence?
column2: Extend or restart: curSum = max(nums[i], curSum + nums[i]). Track maxSum = max(maxSum, curSum). Initialise maxSum = nums[0], not 0.

column1: [Maximum Subarray] Why initialise maxSum to nums[0] instead of 0?
column2: For all-negative arrays, 0 would be wrongly returned — the best subarray is the least-negative element, not an empty subarray.

column1: [Maximum Product Subarray] Why track both curMax and curMin, and what are the three candidates each step?
column2: A negative curMin × negative element can flip to the new maximum. Three candidates: nums[i], nums[i]*prevMax, nums[i]*prevMin. curMax = max of three; curMin = min of three.

column1: [Maximum Product Subarray vs Maximum Subarray] What makes product harder than sum?
column2: Negatives flip sign — a very negative product can become the largest when multiplied by another negative. For sum only curMax matters; for product you need both curMax and curMin.

column1: [Find Minimum in Rotated Sorted Array] What are the two cases in binary search and what do you do in each?
column2: If nums[l] <= nums[r]: window is sorted, minimum is at l (r = mid). If nums[l] > nums[r]: rotation detected — if nums[mid] >= nums[l], mid is in the left section (l = mid+1); else mid is in the right section (r = mid).

column1: [Find Minimum in Rotated Sorted Array] How do you tell which sorted section mid is in when rotation is detected?
column2: Compare nums[mid] with nums[l]: if nums[mid] >= nums[l], mid is on the higher left section — minimum is further right. Otherwise mid is on the lower right section — minimum is at mid or to its left.

column1: [Find Minimum in Rotated Sorted Array] Why use r = mid instead of r = mid - 1 when narrowing right?
column2: Mid itself could be the minimum — discarding it with mid-1 would lose the answer. Use r = mid to keep it in range.

column1: [Search in Rotated Sorted Array] What is the two-step approach to searching in a rotated array?
column2: Step 1: Find the inflection point (minimum index) using rotated binary search. Step 2: Use target >= nums[0] to pick the correct sorted half, then run standard binary search on it.

column1: [Search in Rotated Sorted Array] How do you decide which subarray to search after finding the inflection point?
column2: If target >= nums[0], target is in the left section [0, inflection-1]. Otherwise it's in the right section [inflection, n-1]. Edge case: if inflection is 0 (no rotation), search the full array.

column1: [Search in Rotated Sorted Array vs Find Minimum in Rotated Sorted Array] How does this problem build on LC 153?
column2: The inflection-point logic is identical to LC 153. Once you have the pivot index, the problem reduces to a standard binary search on the correct sorted half.

column1: [3Sum] What is the O(n²) approach — how do you reduce the brute-force O(n³)?
column2: Sort the array. Fix each i as one element (target = -nums[i]), then use two pointers l=i+1 and r=n-1 to find the pair summing to target in O(n). Repeat for all i.

column1: [3Sum] How do you prevent duplicate triplets across all three positions?
column2: Three skips: (1) skip i if nums[i] == nums[i-1]; (2) after a match, advance l past all elements equal to nums[l]; (3) retreat r past all elements equal to nums[r].
